coredata.dc:title: Rapid synthesis of ultra-bright blue and cyan CQDs fluorescent powders based on chemical dispersion and concentration effects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Kun
Lin, Li
Zhang, Li
Zhao, Maojun
Dai, Xianxiang
Jiang, Yuanyuan
Yang, Ruiwu
Liao, Jinqiu
Zhang, Yunsong
Wang, Ying
coredata.dc:description: It is often impossible to obtain solid carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fluorescent powder by direct drying due to a self-quenching effect, which limits their large-scale application. Here, we first report a general method for rapidly conversing CQDs solution into fluorescent powder via a crosslinking reaction of 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560). Initially, N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with an ultra-high quantum yield (QY) of 98.45 % and concentration-dependent luminescence behavior were synthesized via a straightforward hydrothermal reaction involving citric acid and ethylenediamine. The N-CQDs solution at low and high concentrations emits blue and cyan fluorescence under ultraviolet light, respectively. By the action of aqueous ammonia, KH-560 quickly forms a gel matrix at room temperature to realize the dispersion and anchor of N-CQDs to prepare N-CQDs fluorescent powder (NK). NK not only inherits the strong luminescence performance (QY = 71.87 %) of N-CQDs but also exhibits excellent fluorescence stability, successfully applied in blue and cyan LED lighting and fluorescent ink.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
KH-560
Fluorescent powder
Silane

Applications of NK in LED and fluorescent ink
Structural characterization of N-CQDs and NK
Applications of NK in LED lighting and fluorescent ink

China ( LZJ1805 ), Sichuan science and technology plan project of International Cooperation, P.R. China ( 2011ZR0067 ), and the start-up grants of Sichuan Agricultural University for talent, P.R. China ( 03120313 ). We are grateful to the reviewers for their invaluable feedback and comments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (insert: high-resolution), (b) dimensional distribution, and (c) XRD spectrum of N-CQDs; (d) SEM image and (e–h) elemental mapping images of NK-10.
(a) XPS spectrum, (b–e) high-resolution spectra of C 1s, O 1s, Si 2p, and N 1s of NK-10, and (f) FTIR spectrum of NK-10.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, emission spectra, and (b) excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs (0.01 g/L); (c) Concentration-dependent fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs under 365 nm, inset: images of low (0.01 g/L, Ⅰ and Ⅱ) and high (0.4 g/L, Ⅲ and Ⅳ) concentrations of N-CODs solution under daylight and UV (365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NK loading varying concentrations of N-CQDs under 365 nm; (b) Pictures of NK-10 (Ⅰ, Ⅱ) and NK-25 (Ⅲ, Ⅳ) under sunlight and ultraviolet (365 nm); (c) Fluorescence spectra of NK-10 and centrifuged solution from NK-10 immersed in water for 24 h, inset: daylight (Ⅴ) and ultraviolet (Ⅵ, 365 nm) images of NK-10 immersed in water. (d) Fluorescence intensity of NK-10 after continuous excitation under ultraviolet (365 nm, I0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity, and I after continuous excitation).

(a) CIE chromaticity charts of NK-10 and NK-25; (b) Images of LED lamps prepared with NK-10 (Ⅰ and Ⅲ) and NK-25 (Ⅱ and Ⅳ); (c) Images of letters “A" (NK-10) and “B" (NK-25) under daylight (Ⅴ and Ⅵ) and ultraviolet (Ⅶ and Ⅷ, 365 nm).
The preparation process of ultra-bright blue and cyan NK fluorescent powder.
Reported QY of solid-state blue luminescent CQDs.

head.ce:title: Rapid synthesis of ultra-bright blue and cyan CQDs fluorescent powders based on chemical dispersion and concentration effects

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Yaan, 625014, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Yaan 625014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Yaan, 625014, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Yaan 625014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Water Conservancy and Hydropower Engineering, Sichuan Agricultural University, Yaan, 625014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Water Conservancy and Hydropower Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Water Conservancy and Hydropower Engineering, Sichuan Agricultural University, Yaan 625014, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 46 Xinkang Road, Yaan, 625014, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is often impossible to obtain solid carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fluorescent powder by direct drying due to a self-quenching effect, which limits their large-scale application. Here, we first report a general method for rapidly conversing CQDs solution into fluorescent powder via a crosslinking reaction of 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560). Initially, N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with an ultra-high quantum yield (QY) of 98.45 % and concentration-dependent luminescence behavior were synthesized via a straightforward hydrothermal reaction involving citric acid and ethylenediamine. The N-CQDs solution at low and high concentrations emits blue and cyan fluorescence under ultraviolet light, respectively. By the action of aqueous ammonia, KH-560 quickly forms a gel matrix at room temperature to realize the dispersion and anchor of N-CQDs to prepare N-CQDs fluorescent powder (NK). NK not only inherits the strong luminescence performance (QY = 71.87 %) of N-CQDs but also exhibits excellent fluorescence stability, successfully applied in blue and cyan LED lighting and fluorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent powders play an indispensable role in various fields, including lighting [1], anti-counterfeiting [2], bioimaging [3], sensing [4], and more. The preparation of fluorescent powders currently relies predominantly on inorganic metal elements [5–7], but the high cost, stringent reaction conditions, and environmental unfriendliness limit their practical applications [8]. Consequently, research on novel fluorescent powder materials has been a hot spot worldwide.

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant prominence serving as novel luminescent nanoparticles in recent years, primarily owing to their exceptional controllable fluorescence properties, high photostability, low production costs, and excellent biocompatibility [9,10]. CQDs-based fluorescent powders have also been successfully applied in fingerprint identification [11], LED lighting [12], and fluorescent inks [13]. Unfortunately, self-quenching effects among CQDs in their aggregated state resulting from resonance energy transfer and direct π-π interactions typically [14] hinder direct conversion of CQDs solution into CQDs fluorescent powders through simple drying. This has influenced the large-scale application of CQDs as solid-state fluorescent materials.

To suppress self-quenching effects, CQDs fluorescent powders are commonly prepared using two methods: designing molecular structure and employing a matrix for dispersion [15]. For example, Shen et al. introduced a boron element as a dopant to limit the intermolecular charge transfer, thus obtaining CQDs with solid-state fluorescence [16]. Shen et al. in-situ embedded CQDs within a trisodium citrate crystal matrix to synthesize fluorescent CQDs powder [17]. However, the introduction of other elements and the formation of crystal matrixes require ingenious experimental design and demanding reaction conditions. Currently, there is a lack of simpler and more universally applicable approaches for converting solution-state luminescent CQDs into solid-state fluorescent powders.

Silanes are widely acknowledged for being productive coupling agents, widely employed for adhesive formulations and enhancing the performance of composite materials [18]. Recently, 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane (KH-560) has gained attention as a harmless silane agent [19], and it has been reported to have a distinctive epoxy group on one end that is readily susceptible to ring-opening reactions [20] while the silicon-oxygen bond on the other end has also been demonstrated to undergo hydrolysis reactions easily [21]. Based on this, KH-560 has been used as a fixative [22] and crosslinking agent for composite materials [23]. Furthermore, the reaction conditions between the epoxy groups of KH-560 and amino groups are mild [20]. The surface of N-CQDs often contains amino groups [24], which can serve as binding sites for KH-560.

Multicolor luminescence CQDs hold greater appeal and significance in advancing optical applications [25]. As examples, Wang et al. effectively synthesized multicolor biomass CQDs using a solvent control method and applied them in the field of optical anti-counterfeiting [26]. Hu et al. produced CQDs with a diverse range of colors by adjusting nitrogen doping level and the dimensions of sp2 structure regions, and employed them in LED lighting [27]. Based on existing research, the luminescent behavior of CQDs may be related to concentration, but there is still limited research and reporting on the concentration-dependent behavior of CQDs [28].

In this study, blue and cyan CQDs fluorescent powders were successfully rapidly prepared using the concentration-dependent effect of N-CQDs and the chemical dispersion capabilities of KH-560. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , N-CQDs were fabricated with a high QY by employing citric acid and ethylenediamine, which exhibited concentration-dependent emission behavior under ultraviolet excitation, emitting blue and cyan fluorescence at low and high concentrations, respectively. Then, KH-560 and aqueous ammonia were sequentially mixed with the N-CQDs solution. Under the action of aqueous ammonia, the hydrolysis reaction of methoxy groups and the ring-opening reaction of three-membered rings took place, which led KH-560 to rapidly form a gel matrix consisting of Si–O–Si and C–N–C bonds to prepare ultra-bright blue and cyan fluorescent powders based on the concentration-dependent effects of N-CQDs. These obtained powders were successfully applied in LED lighting and fluorescent ink. This will provide fresh perspectives on the synthesis and utilization of innovative fluorescent powders.

KH-560 (97 %) was offered by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aqueous ammonia (25%–28 %), citric acid, ethylenediamine, H3BO3, H3PO4, CH3COOH, NaOH, and NaCl were all offered by Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (AR, Chengdu, China). The water used in all experiments was deionized water. 365 nm emitting LED chips were provided by Wocan Technology Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China). UV-cured adhesive came from Casarez Technology Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China).

N-CQDs with concentration-dependent emission behavior were prepared via a hydrothermal method. Typically, 1.75 g of citric acid and 0.25 g of ethylenediamine were added to 50 mL of DI water. After 10 min of sonication, the resulting solution was moved to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. Upon cooling, the mixture was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed for 48 h (1000 Da). Finally, purified N-CQDs powder is obtained for further use after 2 days of freeze-drying.

Specifically, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs solution (0.01 g/L) were recorded in environments with varying concentrations of NaCl (0–1.0 M) and pH (ranging from 3 to 11, with buffer solutions prepared using H3BO3, H3PO4, CH3COOH, and NaOH). Similarly, the variations in the fluorescence intensities were recorded after continuous irradiation of N-CQDs solution for 0–50 min under 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent powder via KH-560 crosslinking reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2.5 mL of N-CQDs (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 g/L), was mixed with 5 mL of KH-560 and 0.5 mL of aqueous ammonia. After allowing it to stand at room temperature for 1 h, the resulting solidified N-CQDs-KH-560 gel (NK) was obtained. Subsequently, NK was freeze-dried for 6 h, followed by grinding and sieving to obtain the NK fluorescent powder. These resulting powders were named NK-0/5/10/15/20/25/30.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of NK in LED and fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: The LED devices based on NK were manufactured by thoroughly mixing transparent silicone resin with NK-10 and NK-25 fluorescent powders, applying the mixture onto an LED chip, and solidifying it at 40 °C for 3 h. These devices were powered by an adjustable DC voltage supply at 3.5 V. The application of NK in fluorescent ink involves first writing on paper using UV-cured adhesive, then applying fluorescent powder, and finally curing it under 365 nm for 10 min.

The microscopic structure of N-CQDs was observed by a Talos F200S G2 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) transmission electron microscope (TEM) while the crystalline phase was performed applying Ultima IV (Rigaku, Japan) X-ray diffractometry (XRD). The structural examination of NK was carried out with a JSM-7500F (JEOL, Japan) scanning electron microscope (SEM), and surface element characterization was performed by an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The assessment of elements and valence bond structure of N-CQDs and NK were assessed using a K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) separately and a Nicolet Is5 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR). The UV–vis spectrum of N-CQDs was recorded with an A390 spectrophotometer (Aoyi, China) while the absolute photoluminescence quantum yields (QY) of N-CQDs and NK-10 were measured by an FLS-1000 time resolved and steady state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) under the optimal excitation wavelength of N-CQDs. Finally, all fluorescence measurements were carried out via an LS-55 (PerkinElmer, USA) spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of N-CQDs and NK
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 1 a, N-CQDs observed in the TEM image exhibit nearly globular shapes. Besides, the image insert of Fig. 1a reveals a measured lattice fringe distance of 0.21 nm, which is indicative of the crystal face (100) of graphene [29]. Through statistical analysis of these nanoparticles in Fig. 1b, a size range spanning 1.3–6.1 nm was determined, along with a mean size of 3.1 ± 1.1 nm. XRD pattern exhibits a broad peak centered at 22° (Fig. 1c), providing confirmation of the amorphous nature of N-CQDs [30]. XPS analysis discloses the elemental composition, with C (74.01 %), O (18.91 %), and N (7.08 %) being detected (Fig. S1a). This confirms successful N-element doping, which imparts an exceptionally high QY (98.45 %) to N-CQDs [31], thus establishing their foundation as ultra-bright fluorescent powders. The findings derived from high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S1b-d), FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2) and elemental mapping image (Fig. S3d) further demonstrate the presence of N-related structures.

The SEM image (Fig. 1d) depicts NK-10 powder consisting of particles with an average size of approximately 250 μm. Elemental mapping images in Fig. 1e–h further demonstrate the uniform distribution of elements C, O, Si, and N, confirming the successful preparation of NK gel particles based on KH-560. In addition, the micromorphology of NK-10 aligns with that of NK-0 (Fig. S4). Notably, there is an absence of irregular rough structures, unlike the dried N-CQD particles (Fig. S3a). This provides evidence that the incorporation of N-CQDs does not destroy the structure of KH-560 gel but rather results in an even dispersion.

The XPS and FTIR analyses were conducted on NK to gain a deeper understanding of NK's structure. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the XPS spectrum confirms the elemental composition of C, O, Si, and N in NK-10, with respective contents of 66.51 %, 23.6 %, 7.47 %, and 2.42 %. In comparison to the XPS spectrum of NK-0 in Fig. S5a, there is an increase in the N content, which is attributed to the achieved loading of N-CQDs in KH-560 gel. Concretely, the C 1s curve (Fig. 2b) exhibits fitted peaks at 284.2 eV (C–Si), 284.8 eV (C–C/CC), 285.4 eV (C–N), and 286.5 eV (C–O/CO) [32,33]. Meanwhile, the O 1s curve (Fig. 3 c) displays distinctive peaks at 532.0 eV (C–O–C/C–O–H), 532.6 eV (Si–O–C), and 533.2 eV (CO/SiO2 gel) [34,35]. Additionally, the two peaks appear in Fig. 2d at 102.2 eV and 103.0 eV, matching the unreacted Si–O–C bond and the formation of the Si–O–Si bond following hydrolysis of KH-560, respectively [36]. Finally, there are two peaks in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e): the first one, at 399.5 eV, corresponds to the N–H bond, while the second peak, recorded at 401.15 eV, is associated with the C–N–C bond [37]. According to existing reports, the –NH3 on N-CQDs can readily react with the epoxy ring of KH-560 to form C–N–C bonds, achieving stable anchoring of N-CQDs [38]. Worth noting, Fig. S5b also verifies the existence of the C–N–C structure in NK-0, confirming the occurrence of the reaction between aqueous ammonia and the epoxy ring of KH-560, which can promote the formation of a gel matrix. In this experiment, KH-560 is in excess relative to other components, allowing N-CQDs to achieve stable dispersion via the C–N–C bond.

In Fig. 2f, the FTIR spectrum further confirms the results obtained from XPS. The broad peak at approximately 3437 cm−1 can be corresponded to O–H/N–H, while two peaks at 2867 and 2939 cm−1 are associated with the antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –CH2. Besides, the stretching vibrations of various bonds are evident in the FTIR spectrum. Specifically, at 1655, 1202, and 1103 cm−1, the stretching vibrations of CO, C–N, and C–O bonds are observed. At last, three peaks at 1025, 784, and 670 cm−1 are associated with the Si–O–Si bond [21,33]. All of these indicate that based on the crosslinking reaction of KH-560 in an alkaline condition, NK fluorescent powder was successfully prepared.

To delve deeper into the performance of N-CQDs as ultra-bright fluorescent materials, additional analysis of optical properties was conducted. A distinct absorption signal at 334 nm is evident in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3a), indicative of the n-π* transition of the p-π orbit between the graphitic N/C–O and conjugate structure [39], which can lead to strong fluorescence emission [40]. Additionally, N-CQDs can be excited at 330 nm, resulting in a corresponding emission wavelength of 426 nm with a fluorescence lifetime of 15.04 ns (Fig. S6), indicating their capability for emitting blue fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation and possessing a broad emission range. Fig. 3b shows that N-CQDs possess excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior, possibly arising from the formation of multiple emission sites or defect sites through surface groups (-NH2, –OH, and –COOH) [41]. The N-CQDs exhibit their optimal excitation at 330 nm, which is consistent with the UV–vis absorption spectrum results. Furthermore, an investigation into the luminescent stability of N-CQDs was conducted. According to Fig. S7a and b, N-CQDs remains remarkably stable emission even in high ionic condition and extended excitation periods. Fig. S7c illustrates that the fluorescence intensity diminishes under acidic conditions because of changes in protonation state of N-CQDs [42], whereas it exhibits less influence in alkaline conditions. These findings collectively indicate that N-CQDs feature exceptional fluorescence stability.

Interestingly, N-CQDs exhibit concentration-dependent emission behavior. As the N-CQD concentration grew from 0.01 g/L to 0.6 g/L, there was a shift in the maximum emission wavelength from 434 nm to 488 nm (Fig. 3c). The insets in Fig. 3c demonstrate that under 365 nm excitation, low-concentration N-CQD solution emits blue fluorescence, while high-concentration solution emits cyan fluorescence. This dependence on concentration results from the enhancement of the conjugation degree and extension of the electron system during aggregation, causing the energy gaps of N-CQDs to narrow with higher concentrations and leading to a corresponding emission redshift [28,43]. Additionally, the self-quenching effect of N-CQDs also amplifies with the concentration, causing the fluorescence intensity to decrease after reaching 0.1 g/L [41].

Based on the concentration-dependent luminescent behavior of N-CQDs, the fluorescence performance of NK was studied while being loaded with varying concentrations of N-CQDs (Fig. 4 a). The NK-0 loading without N-CQDs exhibits almost no fluorescence. With the increasing concentration, the fluorescence levels in NK-5 to NK-30 initial rise, followed by a subsequent decline due to self-quenching resulting from the clustering of N-CQDs [41] while maximum excitation wavelength shifts from 435 to 488 nm, which is consistent with N-CQDs. These pictures of NK-10 and NK-25 in Fig. 4b further confirm the ability to prepare blue and cyan fluorescent powder by regulating the concentration of loaded N-CQDs. Due to the strong anchoring of N-CQDs via C–N–C bonds within the KH-560 gel, the N-CQDs in NK exhibit minimal detachment in water (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities remain nearly unchanged after continuous excitation for 50 min (Fig. 4d). The above results indicate that NK possesses excellent water resistance and fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of NK in LED lighting and fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, to confirm the universality of our approach in preparing CQDs fluorescent powders using KH-560, we referred to the method by Wang et al. [48], resulting in the synthesis of orange fluorescent CQDs (O-CQDs) dissolved in ethanol. The O-CQDs fluorescent powder (NK-Orange) prepared using this method was also successfully applied in LED lighting and fluorescent ink (Fig. S8a-d).

In summary, blue and cyan fluorescent powders were successfully prepared through the chemical dispersion of N-CQDs using KH-560 gel, along with adjustments to the N-CQDs concentration. KH-560 underwent hydrolysis and ring-opening reactions in the presence of aqueous ammonia to generate a gel framework structure consisting of Si–O–Si and C–N–C bonds, which overcame the self-quenching effect of N-CQDs. N-CQDs were effectively immobilized within the gel matrix via the C–N–C structure, ensuring they remained firmly bound and exhibited excellent fluorescence stability. The N-CQDs dispersed in KH-560 gel retained a high QY, and by exploiting the concentration-dependent emission behavior, fluorescent powders emitting in blue and cyan colors were successfully prepared loading N-CQDs at different concentrations, suitable for LED lighting and fluorescent ink applications. Furthermore, this method is also applicable to CQDs dissolved in ethanol, offering a novel approach for rapidly converting carbon quantum dot solutions into highly luminescent fluorescent powders.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Natural Science Foundation for Youth Project of Sichuan Province (2023NSFSC1121), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21607109), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry of Sichuan Institutes of Higher Education, P.R. China (LZJ1805), Sichuan science and technology plan project of International Cooperation, P.R. China (2019YFH0071, 2016HH0081), the Basic Research Program of the Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province, P.R. China (2011ZR0067), and the start-up grants of Sichuan Agricultural University for talent, P.R. China (03120313). We are grateful to the reviewers for their invaluable feedback and comments.
